title: Two-step inequality word problems
description: Practice constructing, interpreting, and solving linear inequalities that model real-world situations.
topic_path: Topic B: Solve problems using expressions, equations, and inequalities

assessment_items.question_content:
You downloaded a video game to your computer.  You have a $60$-minute free trial of the game. It takes $5\dfrac{1}{6}$ minutes to set up the game and $7\dfrac{1}{3}$ minutes to play each level.  You want to find out how many levels you can play for free.

**Which inequality describes the number of levels, $\ell$, you can play in  $60$ minutes?**  

[[☃ radio 2]]

**Can you play $6$ levels during your free trial?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
You have a $60$-minute free trial of the game.

Can your total time be. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $60$ minutes? | **Yes**
Exactly $60$ minutes? | **Yes**
More than $60$ minutes? | No
Conclusion| Total time $\maroonD{\leq} 60$ minutes

You will spend $\blueD{5 \dfrac{1}{6}}$ minutes setting up the game, then $7 \dfrac{1}{3}$ minutes for each level you play, so you will spend $\greenD {\left(7 \dfrac{1}{3}\right)\ell}$ minutes playing $\ell$ levels.  Together, you will spend $\blueD{5 \dfrac{1}{6}}+\greenD{\left(7 \dfrac{1}{3}\right)\ell}$ minutes.

This must be *less than or equal to* $\purpleC{60}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{5 \dfrac{1}{6}}+\greenD{\left(7 \dfrac{1}{3}\right)\ell}\maroonD{\leq}\purpleC{60}$

Let's substitute in $\goldE{\ell}=\goldE{6}$ to test whether the inequality is true if you play $6$ levels.

$\begin{align}
5 \dfrac{1}{6}+\left(7 \dfrac{1}{3}\right)\goldE{\ell}&\leq 60 \\\\
5 \dfrac{1}{6}+\left(7 \dfrac{1}{3}\right)\goldE{6}&\stackrel{?}{\leq} 60 \\\\
5 \dfrac{1}{6}+44&\stackrel{?}{\leq} 60 \\\\
49 \dfrac{1}{6}  &\stackrel{\checkmark}{\leq} 60
\end{align}$

Substituting in $\goldE{\ell}=\goldE{6}$ makes the inequality true, so you can play $6$ levels during your free trial.

The inequality is:

$5\dfrac{1}{6}+\left(7\dfrac{1}{3}\right)\ell\le 60$

Yes, you can play $6$ levels during your free trial.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tatiana wants to give friendship bracelets to her $32$ classmates.  She already has $5$ bracelets, and she can buy more bracelets in packages of $4$. 

**Write an inequality to determine the number of packages, $p$, Tatiana could buy to have enough bracelets.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Will Tatiana have enough bracelets if she buys $5$ packages?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Tatiana wants to give friendship bracelets to her $32$ classmates.

Can Tatiana give each classmate a bracelet if she has. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $32$ bracelets? | No
Exactly $32$ bracelets? | **Yes**
More than $32$ bracelets? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total bracelets $\maroonD{\geq} 32$

Tatiana already has $\blueD{5}$ bracelets, and gets $p$ packages with $\greenD{4}$ bracelets per package, so there will be $\greenD {4}p$ bracelets in the packages.  Therefore, she has a total of $\blueD5+\greenD{4}p$ bracelets.

This must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{32}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{5}+\greenD{4}p\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{32}$

Let's substitute in $\goldE{p}=\goldE{5}$ to test whether the inequality is true if Tatiana buys $5$ packages.

$\begin{align}
5+4\goldE{p} &\geq 32 \\\\
5+4\goldE{(5)} &\stackrel{?}\geq32 \\\\
5+20 &\stackrel{?}\geq32 \\\\
25 &\ngeq32
\end{align}$

Substituting in $\goldE{p}=\goldE{5}$ makes the inequality false, so Tatiana will not have enough bracelets if she buys $5$ packages.

The inequality is:

$5+4p\geq 32$

No, Tatiana will not have enough bracelets if she buys $5$ packages.

assessment_items.question_content:
Renna pushes the elevator button, but the elevator does not move.  The mass limit for the elevator is $450$ kilograms ($\text{kg}$), but Renna and her load of identical packages mass a total of $620\,\text{kg}$.  Each package has a mass of $37.4\,\text{kg}$.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of packages, $p$,  Renna could remove from the elevator to meet the mass requirement.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the minimum whole number of packages Renna needs to remove from the elevator to meet the mass requirement?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] packages
hints:
The mass limit for the elevator is $450\,\text{kg}$.

Will Renna meet the mass limit if the total mass is. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $450\,\text{kg}$? | **Yes**
Exactly $450\,\text{kg}$? | **Yes**
More than $450\,\text{kg}$? | No
Conclusion| Total mass $\maroonD{\leq} 450\,\text{kg}$

Renna and her load of identical packages weigh a total of $\blueD{620}\,\text{kg}$. Each of the packages masses $\greenD{37.4}\,\text{kg}$, so the mass of $p$ packages is $\greenD{37.4}p\,\text{kg}$.  Therefore, after Renna removes $p$ packages from the elevator, the total mass of Renna and the remaining packages will be:

$\blueD{620}-\greenD{37.4}p$

This must be *less than or equal to* $\purpleC{450}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{620}-\greenD{37.4}p\maroonD{\leq}\purpleC{450}$

Let's start by subtracting $\blueD{620}$ from both sides of the inequality.  Remember that  when we divide an inequality by a negative number, the inequality sign *reverses*.

$\begin{align}
620 - 37.4p &\leq 450 \\\\
620 -\blueD{620} - 37.4p &\leq 450 -\blueD{620}\\\\
-37.4p &\leq -170 \\\\
\dfrac{-37.4p}{\green{-37.4}} &\redD{\geq} \dfrac{-170}{\green{-37.4}} \\\\
p &\redD{\geq} \dfrac{-170}{-37.4} \\\\
p &\redD{\geq} 4\dfrac{6}{11}
\end{align}$

Because Renna cannot remove $\dfrac{6}{11}$ of a package from the elevator, this means that she needs to remove $5$ or more packages to meet the mass requirement.  So Renna needs to remove a minimum of $5$ packages.

The inequality is:

$620-37.4p\le 450$

Renna needs to remove a minimum of $5$ packages from the elevator to meet the mass requirement.

answers.message: $p$ has to be a whole number. Renna cannot split the packages into pieces.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kim's softball team was playing in the championship game.  When there were $4$ innings left, the team was losing by a score of $17$ to $6$ runs.  In the last $4$ innings, her team scored the same number of runs per inning, and the other team did not score  any more runs. Kim's team won with the most runs.

**Write an  inequality to determine the number of runs per inning, $p$, Kim's team could have scored.**

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Find the minimum whole number of runs per inning Kim's team could have scored.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] runs per inning
hints:
Kim's team needed more than $17$ runs to win.

Would Kim's team win with . . | Response
-|-
Less than $17$ runs? | No
Exactly $17$ runs? | No
More than $17$ runs? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total runs $\maroonD{>} 17$

Kim's team already had $\blueD{6}$ runs.    Since they scored $p$ runs per inning for $\greenD{4}$ innings, they scored an additional $\green{4}p$ runs, so Kim's team had a total of $\blueD6+\greenD{4}p$ runs.

This must be *greater than* $\purpleC{17}$, so the inequality is:

$\blue6+\greenD{4}p\maroonD>\purpleC{17}$

We can start by subtracting $\blueD{6}$ from both sides of the inequality:

$\begin{align}
6 + 4p &> 17 \\\\
6- \blueD{6}+ 4p &> 17- \blueD{6}\\\\
4p &> 11 \\\\
\dfrac{4p}{\greenD{4}} &> \dfrac{11}{\greenD{4}}\\
\\
p &> 2\dfrac34
\end{align}$

Kim's team must score *more* than $2\dfrac34$ runs per inning to win the game. So Kim's team must have scored a minimum of $3$ runs per inning.

The inequality is:

$6+4p>17$

Kim's team scored a minimum of $3$ runs per inning.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have $\$20$ to spend on taxi fare.  The ride costs $\$5$ plus $\$2.50$ per kilometer.  

**Write an inequality to determine the distance in kilometers, $d$, you can ride for $\$20$.**  

 [[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the maximum distance, in kilometers, you can ride for $\$20?$**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilometers
hints:
You have $\$20$ to spend on taxi fare.

Can you afford to spend . . | Response
-|-
Less than $\$20$? | **Yes**
Exactly $\$20$? | **Yes**
More than $\$20$? | No
Conclusion| Total dollars $\maroonD{\leq} 20$

The total cost of the taxi is $\blueD{\$5}$ plus the amount it costs to go $d$ kilometers at $\greenD{\$2.50}$ per kilometer, which is $\greenD{2.5}d$.

The total cost of riding the taxi $d$ kilometers is $\blueD5+\greenD{2.5}d$ dollars.

This amount must be *less than or equal to* $\purpleC{20}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD5+\greenD{2.5}d\maroonD{\leq}\purpleC{20}$

To solve, let's start by subtracting $\blueD{5}$ from both sides of the inequality:

$\begin{align}
5+2.5d&\leq20\\\\
5+2.5d -\blueD{5} &\leq20- \blueD{5} \\\\
2.5d &\leq 15\\
\\
\dfrac{2.5d}{\greenD{2.5}} &\leq \dfrac{15}{\greenD{2.5}}\\\\
d &\leq6
\end{align}$

You can ride a maximum of $6$ kilometers.

The inequality is:

$5 + 2.5d  \leq 20$ 

You can ride a maximum of $6$ kilometers for $\$20$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Morton has a special reward system for her class.  When all her students behave well, she rewards them by putting $3$ marbles into a marble jar.  When the jar has  $100$ or more marbles, the students have a party. Right now, the jar has $24$ marbles.  

**Write an inequality to determine the number of additional times, $r$, Mrs. Morton could reward the class in order for the students to have a party.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Will the students have a party if Mrs. Morton rewards them $31$ additional times?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When the jar has  $100$ or more marbles, the students have a party.

Will the students have a party if the jar has. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $100$ marbles? | No
Exactly $100$ marbles? | **Yes**
More than $100$ marbles? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total marbles $\maroonD{\geq} 100$

The jar in Mrs. Morton's class has $\blueD{24}$ marbles, plus the number of marbles for $r$ rewards at $3$ marbles per reward, so the number of marbles added for $r$ rewards is $\green{3}r$.

After the students earn $r$ rewards, the jar has a total of $\blueD{24}+\greenD{3}r$ marbles.

The number of marbles must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{100}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{24}+\greenD{3}r\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{100}$

Let's substitute in $\goldE{r}=\goldE{31}$ to test whether the inequality is true if Mrs. Morton rewards the students $31$ additional times.

$\begin{align}
24+3\goldE{r}&\geq100 \\\\
24+3\cdot\goldE{31}&\stackrel{?}\geq100 \\\\
24+93&\stackrel{?}\geq100 \\\\
117&\stackrel{\checkmark}\geq100 \\\\
\end{align}$

Substituting in $\goldE{r}=\goldE{31}$ makes the inequality true, so Mrs. Morton could reward the class $31$ additional times.

The inequality is:

$24 + 3r \geq 100$

Yes, the students will have a party if Mrs. Morton rewards them $31$ additional times.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joey Chestnut is trying to break his own record for eating the most whole hot dogs in $10$ minutes.  He needs to eat more than $72$ hot dogs to break his record. After $1$ minute, Joey has eaten $11 \dfrac{1}{4}$ hot dogs.  Let $d$ represent the number of hot dogs Joey eats per minute for the next $9$ minutes.

**Which inequality represents the number of hot dogs per minute Joey could eat for the last $9$ minutes to break his record?**  

[[☃ radio 2]]
 
**Will Joey break his record if he eats $6$ hot dogs per minute for the next $9$ minutes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Joey needs to eat more than $72$ hot dogs to break his record.

Will Joey break his record if he eats . . | Response
-|-
Less than $72$ hot dogs? | No
Exactly $72$ hot dogs? | No
More than $72$ hot dogs? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total hot dogs $\maroonD{>} 72$

Joey  has already eaten $\blueD{11\dfrac{1}{4}}$ hot dogs. If he eats $d$ hot dogs per minute for $\greenD{9}$ minutes, he will have eaten  $\greenD{9}d$ additional hot dogs.  The total number of hot dogs is then:

$\blueD{11\dfrac{1}{4}}+\greenD{9}d$

This must be *greater than* $\purpleC{72}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{11\dfrac{1}{4}}+\greenD{9}d\maroonD{>}\purpleC{72}$

Let's substitute in $\goldE{d}=\goldE{6}$ to test whether the inequality is true if Joey eats $6$ hot dogs per minute.

$\begin{align}
11\dfrac{1}{4}+9\goldD{d}&>72\\\\
11\dfrac{1}{4}+9 \cdot \goldD{6}&\stackrel{?}{>}72\\\\
11\dfrac{1}{4}+54&\stackrel{?}{>}72\\\\
65\dfrac{1}{4}&\ngtr72
\end{align}$

Substituting in $\goldE{d}=\goldE{6}$ makes the inequality false, so Joey will not break his record if he eats $6$ hot dogs per minute.

The inequality is: 

$11\dfrac{1}{4}+9d>72$

No, Joey will not break his record if he eats $6$ hot dogs per minute for the next $9$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Janie has $\$3$.  She earns $\$1.20$ for each chore she does and can do fractions of chores.  She wants to earn enough money to buy a CD for $\$13.50$.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of chores, $c$, Janie could do to have enough money to buy the CD.**

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Graph the solution set to this inequality.**

[[☃ number-line 1]]
hints:
Janey wants to earn enough money to buy a CD for $\$13.50$.

Can Janey buy the CD if she has. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $\$13.50$? | No
Exactly $\$13.50$? | **Yes**
More than $\$13.50$? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total dollars $\maroonD{\geq} 13.50$

If Janie does $c$ chores, she will earn $\greenD{1.2}c$ dollars for doing chores, plus $\blueD{3}$ dollars she already has.  The amount she has is :

$\blueD{3}+\greenD{1.2}c$

This amount must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{13.50}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{3}+\greenD{1.2c}\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{13.50}$

To solve the inequality, let's start by subtracting $\blueD{3}$ from both sides of the inequality:

$\begin{align}
3+1.2c &≥ 13.5\\\\
3+1.2c -\blueD{3} &≥ 13.5- \blueD{ 3}\\\\
1.2c &≥ 10.5
\\\\
\dfrac{1.2c}{\greenD{1.2}} &≥ \dfrac{10.5}{\greenD{1.2}}\\
\\
c &≥ 8.75
\end{align}$

Janie must do $8.75$ or more chores to have enough money to purchase the CD.

To graph the inequality $c\geq 8.75$, we first draw a circle at $8.75$.  This circle divides the number line into two sections: one that contains solutions to the inequality and one that does not.  

The solution  includes the point $c=8.75$, so we *fill in* the circle at $8.75$.

Because the solution to the inequality says that $c\geq 8.75$, this means that solutions are numbers to the *right* of $8.75$.

The inequality is:

$3+1.2c\geq 13.50$

The graph of the solution of the inequality, $c ≥ 8.75$, looks like this:

[[☃ number-line 3]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Katie wants to collect over $100$ seashells. She already has $34$ seashells in her collection. Each day, she finds $12$ more seashells on the beach.  Katie can use fractions of days to find seashells.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of days, $d$, it will take  Katie to collect over $100$ seashells.**

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Graph the solution set to this inequality.**

[[☃ number-line 1]]
hints:
Katie wants to collect over $100$ seashells.

Does Katie want to collect. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $100$ seashells? |No
Exactly $100$ seashells? | No
More than $100$ seashells? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total shells $\maroonD{>} 100$

Katie already has $\blueD{34}$ seashells. If she collects $\greenD{12}$ seashells per day for $d$ more days, she will have $\greenD{12}d$ additional seashells.

After $d$ days, Katie will have a total of $\blueD{34}+\greenD{12}d$ seashells in her collection.

This amount must be *greater than* $\purpleC{100}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{34}+\greenD{12}d\maroonD{>}\purpleC{100}$

To solve the inequality, let’s start by subtracting $\blueD{34}$ from both sides:

$\begin{align}
34 + 12d &> 100\\\\
34- \blueD{34} + 12d &> 100- \blueD{34}\\\\
12d &> 66\\\\
\dfrac{12d}{\greenD{12}} &> \dfrac{66}{\greenD{12}}\\
\\
d &> 5\dfrac{1}{2}
\end{align}$

Katie must collect seashells for more than $5\dfrac{1}{2}$ days.

To graph the inequality $d>5\dfrac{1}{2}$, we first **draw a circle at  $5\dfrac{1}{2}$**.  This circle divides the number line into two sections: one that contains solutions to the inequality and one that does not.  

The solution does not include the point $d=5\dfrac{1}{2}$, so the circle at $5\dfrac{1}{2}$ is **not filled in**.

Because the solution to the inequality says that $d>5\dfrac{1}{2}$, this means that solutions are numbers to the **right** of $5\dfrac{1}{2}$.

The inequality is:

$34+12d>100$

The graph that represents the solution of the inequality,  $d > 5\dfrac{1}{2}$,  looks like this:

[[☃ number-line 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The latest online craze is a new game, Khan on Seven.  You get $100$ points for playing the game. In addition, you get $50$ points for each seven-letter word you make with the ten letters you receive. Sal wants to break the record, and he needs  $18{,}000$ or more points to do so.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of seven-letter words, $w$, Sal could make to break the record.**

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Graph the solution set to this inequality.**

[[☃ number-line 1]]
hints:
Sal wants to break the record, and he needs  $18{,}000$ points or more to do so.

Will Sal break the record if he earns . . . | Response
-|-
Less than $18{,}000$ points? | No
Exactly $18{,}000$ points? | **Yes**
More than $18{,}000$ points? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total points $\maroonD{\geq} 18{,}000$

Sal  gets $\blueD{100}$ points for playing the game, plus $\greenD{50}$ points per word for  $w$ seven-letter words, or $\greenD{50}w$ points. If he makes $w$ seven-letter words, Sal will have a total of $\blueD{100}+\greenD{50}w$ points.  

This must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{18{,}000}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{100}+\greenD{50}w\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{18{,}000}$

To solve the inequality, let’s start by subtracting $\blueD{100}$ from both sides:

$\begin{align}
100+50w &\geq 18{,}000\\\\
100+50w- \blueD{100} &\geq18{,}000- \blueD{100}\\\\
  50w&\geq 17{,}900\\\\
\dfrac{50w}{\greenD{50}} &\geq \dfrac{17{,}900}{\greenD{50}}\\\\
w &\geq 358
\end{align}$

Sal must make at least $358$ seven-letter words to break the record!

To graph the inequality $w\geq358$, we first **draw a circle at  $358$**.  This circle divides the number line into two sections: one that contains solutions to the inequality and one that does not.  

The solution  includes the point $w=358$, so the **circle at $358$ is filled in**.

Because the solution to the inequality says that $w\geq358$, this means that solutions are numbers to the **right** of $358$.

The inequality is:

$100+50w\geq18{,}000$

The graph that represents the solution of the inequality,  $w\geq358$,  looks like this: 

[[☃ number-line 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Darcie wants to crochet a minimum of $3$ blankets to donate to a homeless shelter. Darcie crochets at a rate of $\dfrac{1}{15}$ of a blanket per day. She has $60$ days until when she wants to donate the blankets, but she also wants to skip crocheting some days so she can volunteer in other ways.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of days, $s$, Darcie can skip crocheting and still meet her goal.**

[[☃ expression 1]]

**Graph the solution set to this inequality.**

[[☃ number-line 1]]
hints:
Darcie wants to donate a minimum of $3$ blankets.

Will Darcie meet her goal if she crochets . . | Response
-|-
Less than $3$ blankets? | No
Exactly $3$ blankets? | **Yes**
More than $3$ blankets? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total blankets $\maroonD{\geq} 3$

If Darcie skips $s$ days, then she will crochet for $\goldE{60-s}$ days. Darcie crochets at a rate of $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{15}}$ of a blanket per day, so she can crochet a total of $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{15}}(\goldE{60-s})$ blankets.  

This amount must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{3}$ blankets, so the inequality is:

$\greenD{\dfrac{1}{15}}(\goldE{60-s})\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{3}$

To solve the inequality, let's start by multiplying both sides by $\blueD{15}$.   Remember that  when we multiply an inequality by a negative number, the inequality sign *reverses*.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{15}(60-s)&\geq 3 \\\\
\blueD{15}\cdot \dfrac{1}{15}(60-s)&\geq \blueD{15} \cdot 3 \\\\
60-s &\geq 45 \\\\
60-s \gray{-60} &\geq 45\gray{-60} \\\\
-s &\geq -15 \\\\
-s\tealD{\cdot -1} &\redD{\leq} -15\tealD{\cdot -1}  \\\\
s &\redD{\leq} 15
\end{align}$

Darcie can skip no more than $15$ days of crocheting to meet her goal.

To graph the inequality $s \leq 15$, we first **draw a circle at $15$**.  This circle divides the number line into two sections: one that contains solutions to the inequality and one that does not.  

The solution includes the point $s=15$, so the circle at  $15$ is **filled in**.

Because the solution to the inequality says that $s\leq 15$, this means that solutions are numbers to the **left** of $15$.

The inequality is:

$\dfrac{1}{15}(60-s)\geq 3$

The graph of the solution of the inequality, $s \leq 15$, looks like this:


[[☃ number-line 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Noor brought $21$ sheets of stickers. She gave $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a sheet to each of the $45$ students at recess. She wants to give teachers $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ sheets each. 

**Which inequality describes the number of teachers, $t$, Noor could give sheets of stickers to?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

$t$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Noor brought $21$ sheets of stickers.

Can Noor give away. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $21$ sheets? | **Yes**
Exactly $21$ sheets? | **Yes**
More than $21$ sheets? | No
Conclusion| Total sheets $\maroonD{\leq} 21$

Noor gave $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a sheet to each of the $45$ students, so she gave $\blueD{15}$ sheets to students because $\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot 45=15$. She wants to give each teacher $\greenD{1\dfrac{1}{2}}$ sheets of stickers. She wants to give a total of 
$\greenD{\dfrac{3}{2}}t$ sheets to the $t$ teachers because $\greenD{1\dfrac{1}{2}}\cdot t=\greenD{\dfrac{3}{2}}t$. 

Noor will give away a total of $\blueD{15}+\greenD{\dfrac{3}{2}}t$ sheets of stickers.

This must be *less than or equal to* $\purpleC{21}$ sheets, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{15}+\greenD{\dfrac{3}{2}}t \maroonD{\leq} \purpleC{21}$

Let's start by subtracting $\blueD{15}$ from both sides of the inequality. 

$\begin{align}
15+\dfrac{3}{2}t &\leq 21 \\\\
15+\dfrac{3}{2}t - \blueD{15} &\leq 21 - \blueD{15}\\\\
\dfrac{3}{2}t &\leq 6 \\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{2}{3}}\cdot\dfrac{3}{2}t &\leq \goldE{\dfrac{2}{3}}\cdot 6\\\\
t &\leq 4
\end{align}$

Noor can give sheets of stickers to up to $4$ teachers.

The inequality is: 

$15+\dfrac{3}{2}t\le 21$

The solution set is $t\leq 4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The bottom of Ignacio's desktop is $74.5\,\text{cm}$ from the floor. Ignacio sits in his adjustable chair, and the tops of his legs are $49.3\,\text{cm}$ from the floor. Each clockwise rotation of the knob on the chair raises Ignacio's legs by $4.8\,\text{cm}$.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of clockwise rotations, $r$, Ignacio could make with the knob without his legs touching the desk.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

$r$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The bottom of Ignacio's desktop is $74.5\,\text{cm}$ from the floor. He does not want his legs to touch the bottom of the desk, so he wants the tops of  his legs to be *lower* than the bottom of the desk.

Will Ignacio's legs be lower than the desk at a height of. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $74.5\,\text{cm}$? | **Yes**
Exactly $74.5\,\text{cm}$? | No
More than $74.5\,\text{cm}$? | No
Conclusion| Leg height $\maroonD{<} 74.5$

Ignacio's legs are already $\blueD{49.3}\,\text{cm}$ above the floor. Each rotation raises them by $\greenD{4.8}\,\text{cm}$ more, so $r$ rotations raises them by $\greenD{4.8}r\,\text{cm}$.

After $r$ rotations, Ignatio's legs will be $\blueD{49.3}+\greenD{4.8}r\,\text{cm}$ above the floor.

This must be *less than* $\purpleC{74.5}\,\text{cm}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{49.3}+\greenD{4.8}r \maroonD{<} \purpleC{74.5}$

Let's start by subtracting $\blueD{49.3}$ from both sides of the inequality. 

$\begin{align}
49.3+4.8r &<74.5 \\\\
49.3+4.8r \blueD{-49.3}&<74.5 \blueD{-49.3} \\\\
4.8r&<25.2 \\\\
\dfrac{4.8r}{\greenD{4.8}} &<\dfrac{25.2}{\greenD{4.8}} \\\\
r &< \dfrac{21}{4} \\\\
r &< 5.25
\end{align}$

Ignacio can make less than $5.25$ rotations without his legs touching the desk.

The inequality is:

$49.3+4.8r <74.5$

The solution set is $r < 5.25$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mustafa, Heloise, and Gia have written more than a combined total of $22$ articles for the school newspaper. Heloise has written $\dfrac{1}{4}$ as many articles as Mustafa has. Gia has written $\dfrac{3}{2}$ as many articles as Mustafa has.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of articles, $m$, Mustafa could have written for the school newspaper.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Mustafa, Heloise, and Gia have written more than a combined total of $22$ articles for the school newspaper.

Is the total number of articles. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $22$ articles? | No
Exactly $22$ articles? | No
More than $22$ articles? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total articles $\maroonD{>} 22$

Mustafa has written $m$ articles. Heloise has written $\dfrac{1}{4}m$ articles. Gia has written $\dfrac{3}{2}m$ articles.

They have written a combined total of $\blueD{m+\dfrac{1}{4}m+\dfrac{3}{2}m}$ articles.

This must be *more than* $\purpleC{22}$ articles, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{m+\dfrac{1}{4}m+\dfrac{3}{2}m} \maroonD{>}\purpleC{22}$

Let's start by combining like terms.

$\begin{align}
m+\dfrac{1}{4}m+\dfrac{3}{2}m&>22 \\\\
\dfrac{4}{4}m+\dfrac{1}{4}m+\dfrac{6}{4}m&>22 \\\\
\dfrac{11}{4}m&>22 \\\\
\goldD{\dfrac{4}{11}}\cdot\dfrac{11}{4}m&>\goldD{\dfrac{4}{11}}\cdot 22 \\\\
m &> 8
\end{align}$

Mustafa has written more than $8$ articles.

The inequality is:

$m+\dfrac{1}{4}m+\dfrac{3}{2}m>22$

The solution set is $m>8$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The swimming pool is open when the high temperature is higher than $20^\circ\text{C}$. Lainey tried to swim on Monday and Thursday (which was $3$ days later). The pool was open on Monday, but it was closed on Thursday. The high temperature was $30^\circ\text{C}$ on Monday, but decreased at a constant rate in the next $3$ days.

**Write an inequality to determine the rate, $d$, of temperature decrease in degrees Celsius per day from Monday to Thursday.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

$d$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The swimming pool is open when the high temperature is more than $20^\circ\text{C}$. We want to know about temperatures when the pool is *closed*.

Is the pool *closed* when the temperature is. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $20^\circ\text{C}$? | **Yes**
Exactly $20^\circ\text{C}$? | **Yes**
More than $20^\circ\text{C}$? | No
Conclusion| Temperature $\maroonD{\leq} 20$

The high temperature was $\blueD{30^\circ\text{C}}$ on Monday. The temperature was $d^\circ\text{C}$ cooler each day, so $\greenD{3}$ days later, it was $\greenD{3}d^\circ\text{C}$ cooler than it was on Monday.

Three days after Monday, it was $\blueD{30}-\greenD{3}d^\circ\text{C}$.

This must be *less than or equal to* $\purpleC{20}^\circ\text{C}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{30}-\greenD{3d}\maroonD{\leq}\purpleC{20}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's start by subtracting $\blueD{30}$ from both sides of the inequality.   Remember that  when we divide an inequality by a negative number, the inequality sign *reverses*.

$\begin{align}
30-3d&\leq20 \\\\
30-3d-\blueD{30}&\leq20 -\blueD{30}\\\\
-3d&\leq -10\\\\
\dfrac{-3d}{\goldD{-3}}&\redD{\geq} \dfrac{-10}{\goldD{-3}}\\\\
d&\redD{\geq} \dfrac{10}{3}
\end{align}$

The high temperature decreased by at least $\dfrac{10}{3}$ degrees Celsius per day from Monday to Thursday.

The inequality is:

$30-3d\leq20$

The solution set is $d\geq \dfrac{10}{3}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Shane and Abha earned a team badge that required their team to collect no less than $2000$ cans for recycling. Abha collected $178$ more cans than Shane did.

**Write an inequality to determine the number of cans, $S$, that Shane could have collected.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

$S$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The badge required the team to collect no less than $2000$ cans for recycling.

Can the team earn the badge for collecting. . . | Response
-|-
Less than $2000$ cans? | No
Exactly $2000$ cans? | **Yes**
More than $2000$ cans? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total cans $\maroonD{\geq} 2000$

Shane collected $\blueD{S}$ cans. Abha collected $178$ more cans, so she collected $\greenD{S+178}$ cans.

The team collected a total of $\blueD{S}+\greenD{S+178}$ cans.

This must be *greater than or equal to* $\purpleC{2000}$ cans, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{S}+\greenD{S+178}\maroonD{\geq}\purpleC{2000}$

Let's start by combining like terms.

$\begin{align}
S+S+178 &\geq 2000 \\\\
2S+178 &\geq 2000 \\\\
2S+178\goldE{-178} &\geq 2000\goldE{-178} \\\\
2S &\geq 1822 \\\\
\dfrac{2S}{\redD{2}} &\geq \dfrac{1822}{\redD{2}} \\\\
S &\geq 911
\end{align}$

Shane collected at least $911$ cans for recycling.

The inequality is:

$S+S+178 \geq 2000$

The solution set is $S \geq 911$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The dance committee of Pine Bluff Middle School earns $\$72$ from a bake sale and will earn $\$4$ for each ticket they sell to the Spring Fling dance. The dance will cost $\$400$.

**Write an inequality to determine the number, $t$, of tickets the committee could sell to have money left over after they pay for this year's dance.**  

[[☃ expression 1]]

**What is the solution set of the inequality?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Spring Fling dance will cost $\$400$.

Will there be leftover money if the committee earns . . . | Response
-|-
Less than $\$400$? | No
Exactly $\$400$? | No
More than $\$400$? | **Yes**
Conclusion| Total dollars $\maroonD{>} 400$

The committee has $\blueD{72}$ dollars plus the amount they will earn by selling $t$ tickets at $\greenD{\$4}$ per ticket, which is $\greenD{4}t$ dollars.

The committee will earn a total of $\blueD{72}+\greenD{4}t$ dollars.

This amount  must be *greater than* $\purpleC{400}$, so the inequality is:

$\blueD{72}+\greenD{4}t\maroonD{>}\purpleC{400}$

To solve, let's start by subtracting $\blueD{72}$ from both sides of the inequality:

$\begin{align}
72+4t&>400 \\\\
72+4t -\blueD{72} &> 400- \blueD{72} \\\\
4t &> 328\\\\
\dfrac{4t}{\green{4}} &> \dfrac{328}{\green{4}}\\
\\
t &> 82
\end{align}$

The  committee must sell more than $82$ tickets to have money left over after paying for the dance.

The inequality is:

${72} + 4t > 400$

The solution set is $t> 82$.
